questions.question: What is the best first step after an interviewer changes a major constraint?
options: Restate which original assumption changed and identify the earliest parts of the design it affects.
Replace every core component with a managed service.
Restart the answer from a blank page without preserving context.
Ignore the change until the interviewer asks again.
questions.explanation: Redesign starts with the assumption delta, not the technology swap.

questions.question: Why is a full restart usually weak when constraints change?
options: Because redesign should preserve stable parts unless the prompt truly becomes a different system.
Because interviewers never ask for clean-sheet redesigns.
Because old architectures always survive unchanged.
Because migration plans are not relevant in interviews.
questions.explanation: Good redesign reasoning separates what breaks from what remains valid.

questions.question: Which response best handles a shift from single-region to multi-region?
options: Say the current design is fine because regions only affect deployment.
Call out replication, failover, consistency windows, and which control-plane or data-plane paths must change first.
Add a CDN and leave the storage tier unchanged without discussion.
Skip migration because the new topology is obviously better.
questions.explanation: A strong answer names the concrete impact areas and the resulting trade-offs.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of preserving unchanged parts of the original architecture during redesign?
options: It shows continuity and reduces unnecessary rework in the answer.
It guarantees the redesign will cost less in production.
It avoids the need to discuss any new risks.
It removes the need for capacity estimates.
questions.explanation: Good redesign answers evolve the system rather than pretending everything resets.

questions.question: Which omission most weakens a redesign answer?
options: Not mentioning a migration or rollout path for the changed topology.
Not drawing a new title for the diagram.
Not changing the order of API examples.
Not listing every possible storage engine.
questions.explanation: Migration and rollout are part of realistic redesign thinking.

questions.question: What trade-off often comes with incremental redesign instead of a clean-sheet rewrite?
options: You preserve continuity, but may carry some legacy constraints forward.
You increase consistency and reduce all operational complexity automatically.
You avoid needing any observability changes.
You eliminate the need to re-evaluate failure modes.
questions.explanation: Incremental change is practical, but not free.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals whether redesign practice is getting stronger?
options: How quickly you identify the broken assumption and map it to affected components.
The number of services added after the redesign.
The number of arrows in the new diagram.
The time spent renaming components.
questions.explanation: Fast, precise identification of impact is the key redesign skill.

questions.question: If stronger correctness requirements are introduced, what should you usually discuss next?
options: How write paths, coordination, latency, and failure handling change under the tighter guarantee.
How to add more read replicas for free throughput.
How to shorten the wrap-up by removing trade-offs.
How to keep all previous assumptions unchanged.
questions.explanation: Correctness changes usually land first on write semantics, coordination, and availability trade-offs.
